author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy
book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
On the Morals of the Manichæans.
Actions to Be Judged of from Their Motive, Not from Externals. Manichæan Abstinence to Be Tried by This Principle.

title: Actions to Be Judged of from Their Motive, Not from Externals. Manichæan Abstinence to Be Tried by This Principle.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Having every day in your mouth these blasphemies which come from your heart, you ought not to continue holding up the symbol of the mouth as something wonderful, to ensnare the ignorant.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But perhaps you think the symbol of the mouth excellent and admirable because you do not eat flesh or drink wine.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But what is your end in this?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For according as the end we have in view in our actions, on account of which we do whatever we do, is not only not culpable but also praiseworthy, so only can our actions merit any praise.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: If the end we have regard to in any performance is unlawful and blameworthy, the performance itself will be unhesitatingly condemned as improper.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: We are told of Catiline that he could bear cold, thirst, and hunger.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: This the vile miscreant had in common with our apostles.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: What then distinguishes the parricide from our apostles but the precisely opposite end which he followed?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: He bore these things in order to gratify his fierce and ungoverned passions; they, on the other hand, in order to restrain these passions and subdue them to reason.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: You often say, when you are told of the great number of Catholic virgins, a she-mule is a virgin.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: This, indeed, is said in ignorance of the Catholic system, and is not applicable.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Still, what you mean is that this continence is worthless unless it leads, on right principles, to an end of high excellence.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Catholic Christians might also compare your abstinence from wine and flesh to that of cattle and many small birds, as likewise of countless sorts of worms.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But, not to be impertinent like you, I will not make this comparison prematurely, but will first examine your end in what you do.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For I suppose I may safely take it as agreed on, that in such customs the end is the thing to look to.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Therefore, if your end is to be frugal and to restrain the appetite which finds gratification in eating and drinking, I assent and approve.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But this is not the case.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Suppose, what is quite possible, that there is one so frugal and sparing in his diet, that, instead of gratifying his appetite or his palate, he refrains from eating twice in one day, and at supper takes a little cabbage moistened and seasoned with lard, just enough to keep down hunger; and quenches his thirst, from regard to his health, with two or three draughts of pure wine; and this is his regular diet: whereas another of different habits never takes flesh or wine, but makes an agreeable repast at two o’clock on rare and foreign vegetables, varied with a number of courses, and well sprinkled with pepper, and sups in the same style towards night; and drinks honey-vinegar, mead, raisin-wine, and the juices of various fruits, no bad imitation of wine, and even surpassing it in sweetness; and drinks not for thirst but for pleasure; and makes this provision for himself daily, and feasts in this sumptuous style, not because he requires it, but only gratifying his taste;—which of these two do you regard as living most abstemiously in food and drink?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: You cannot surely be so blind as not to put the man of the little lard and wine above this glutton!

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: This is the true view; but your doctrine sounds very differently.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For one of your elect distinguished by the three symbols may live like the second person in this description, and though he may be reproved by one or two of the more sedate, he cannot be condemned as abusing the symbols.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But should he sup with the other person, and moisten his lips with a morsel of rancid bacon, or refresh them with a drink of spoilt wine, he is pronounced a transgressor of the symbol, and by the judgment of your founder is consigned to hell, while you, though wondering, must assent.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Will you not discard these errors?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Will you not listen to reason?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Will you not offer some little resistance to the force of habit?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Is not such doctrine most unreasonable?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Is it not insanity?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Is it not the greatest absurdity that one, who stuffs and loads his stomach every day to gratify his appetite with mushrooms, rice, truffles, cake, mead, pepper, and assafœtida, and who fares thus every day, cannot be convicted of transgressing the three symbols, that is, the rule of sanctity; whereas another, who seasons his dish of the commonest herbs with some smoky morsel of meat, and takes only so much of this as is needed for the refreshment of his body, and drinks three cups of wine for the sake of keeping in health, should, for exchanging the former diet for this, be doomed to certain punishment?
